flashcards.Back: The use of software to automatically configure, manage, and monitor network devices, reducing manual effort and improving efficiency.
The ability to manage and control network hardware through software and APIs rather than manual CLI interaction.
The traditional process of an engineer typing individual commands on each device one-by-one, which is slow and prone to errors.
A major benefit of automation, ensuring that all devices in a network are configured with identical standard settings without variations.
The decrease in network outages and security holes achieved by using automated scripts instead of manual typing for repetitive tasks.
The ability to manage hundreds or thousands of devices as easily as one, which is essential for large enterprise and cloud environments.
A problem in manual networks where individual devices gradually become inconsistent due to undocumented local changes. automation prevents this.
The improvement in speed and resource usage gained by automating day-to-day management tasks like monitoring and troubleshooting.
The reduction in time required to roll out new network services or configuration changes across the entire infrastructure.
A management philosophy where network configurations are treated like software code, allowing for version control and automated testing.
A model where an administrator interacts with a central software controller, which then automates the configuration of all physical devices.
A software interface that allows different programs (like an automation script and a network controller) to communicate and exchange data.
The use of automated tools to continuously gather performance data and health status from devices without manual polling.
The process of automatically setting up a new network device from scratch, including software installation and base configuration.
A management approach where administrators define high-level business rules, and automation software applies them to the network.
The automated analysis of large volumes of network data to identify trends, predict failures, and optimize performance.
The use of automation to track, test, and apply changes to the network while maintaining a full audit log of all modifications.
An automated feature where a new device can be plugged into the network and automatically download its configuration without any manual CLI work.
The increased uptime and stability of a network that results from consistent, tested, and automated configuration processes.
The practice of using predefined templates for all network configurations, which is enforced and simplified through automation.
The use of automation to manage a device from initial installation through updates and monitoring, until it is decommissioned.
The use of software tools to automatically detect and isolate the root cause of network issues faster than manual investigation.
The automated allocation and optimization of network bandwidth, IP addresses, and hardware resources.
A networking architecture that separates the control plane from the data plane and uses automation for centralized management.
The ability of automation tools (like Ansible) to manage devices from different manufacturers using a single, unified approach.
The practice of storing network configurations in a system like Git, allowing for history tracking and the ability to 'roll back' to a previous state.
An automatically generated record of every configuration change made to the network, including who made it and exactly what was changed.
A standardized way of representing network data (like YANG) so that it can be easily understood and processed by automation software.
The automated checking of device configurations against security and organizational standards to ensure they remain in compliance.
The long-term savings in personnel time and reduced downtime achieved by implementing automated network management systems.
